evidence_quotes.quote: predatory monetisation systems such as microtransactions and lootboxes (de facto masked gambling)
intrusive DRM systems that prevent the enjoyment of the right to private copying
the terms of the EULA are de facto unfair because the use of a game purchased should not be burdened by unfair terms

paragraphs.text: I believe that this law should also apply to video games and I do not understand why it does not already apply to video games. The ligneous cardons which are: — the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, – the Consumer Rights Directive, and – the Unfair Contract Terms Directive. I think video games often fall fully within those terms. Indeed, there are video games sold as if they were products (licensed without a deadline), but they are rendered inoperative by publishers. This is unfair on the commercial side because those who sell video games properly functioning forever have unfair competition, unfair to the rights of consumers because they do not protect their purchases and the terms of the EULA are de facto unfair because the use of a game purchased should not be burdened by unfair terms, including by minors who should be able to play without having to worry about legal terms that they cannot understand or legally accept. In addition, video games that apply these unfair policies are often also guilty of other injustices, such as the implementation of predatory monetisation systems such as microtransactions and lootboxes (de facto masked gambling), very intrusive DRM systems that prevent the enjoyment of the right to private copying (although what is legal is not fair). Of course, these are just some of the examples that the poor regulation of the sector has brought. A system is being created in which the consumer is led to believe that the purchase of a licence is not, as it should be, a way to protect copyright, but rather a way to impose any conditions. The licensed purchase is created in order to protect the copyright right, so the intellectual property of the work is not sold but only a copy is sold for the use and consumption of the private individual. However, this should not interfere with the private ownership of the licence and the licence should not have conditions beyond those possible in a normal transaction of any kind of property a car. If I buy anything, I expect the latter to work until it breaks down. If you buy a video game, the same principle applies. The way certain publishers act is now worse than planned obsolescence, creating games with characteristics that will make them non-play when they decide to do so at any time and for any reason. Ensuring that this law also applies to video games should strongly limit this phenomenon.
